items.q: Which macronutrient provides the most energy per gram?
items.why: Students assume carbs (the 'energy food') provide most energy per gram. Carbs and protein = 4 kcal/g; FAT = 9 kcal/g (most energy-dense). Vitamins provide no energy at all.

items.q: On a traffic-light label, what does a red light indicate?
items.correct: High amounts of that nutrient per 100g - eat less often.
items.misconception: The food is dangerous and should never be eaten.
items.why: Students treat red as 'forbidden'. Traffic lights are a guidance system - red = high, eat less often or in small portions. Many foods (cheese, olive oil) are red on certain nutrients but fine in moderation.
Students confuse fat- and water-soluble vitamins. Mnemonic: ADEK fat-soluble (stored in body fat - can build up to toxic levels). B and C water-soluble (excess excreted in urine).

items.q: Gram for gram, how does the energy in fat compare with sugar?
items.correct: Fat gives 9 kcal per gram - more than double sugar's roughly 4 kcal per gram.
items.misconception: Sugar gives more energy per gram than fat.
items.random_wrong: Both give exactly 7 kcal per gram.
items.why: Anti-sugar headlines make students rank sugar top for calories. The energy values to memorise: fat 9 kcal/g, carbohydrate ~4 kcal/g, protein 4 kcal/g. Fat's density is why small amounts of oil, butter or pastry add many calories.

items.q: Does eating extra protein automatically build extra muscle?
items.correct: No - muscle grows in response to exercise; surplus protein beyond the body's needs is used for energy or stored as fat.
items.misconception: Yes - the more protein you eat, the more muscle your body makes.
items.random_wrong: Protein cannot be used for energy under any circumstances.
items.why: A gym-culture myth. Protein supplies amino acids for growth and repair, but without the stimulus of activity the body does not bank extra muscle. Excess protein is deaminated in the liver and the remainder used for energy or converted to fat.
Correct answer: Starch in potato. “Glucose” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many essential amino acids must adults get from food?
items.why: The real figure is 8. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
